title: Business Entity Taxation
description: Compare tax treatment of different business entities and evaluate entity selection for tax efficiency.
topics: Compare tax treatment of different business entities
Apply the qualified business income deduction
Evaluate entity selection for tax efficiency

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Understanding how different business entities are taxed is essential for advising business owner clients.

sections.title: SE Tax Calculation
sections.content: SE Tax = Net SE Income × 92.35% × 15.3%. Half of SE tax is deductible above the line.

sections.title: S Corp Example
sections.content: S Corp earns $200,000. Owner takes $80,000 salary (subject to FICA 15.3%) and $120,000 distribution (no SE/FICA tax). Savings: $120,000 × 15.3% = $18,360 in SE tax avoided.

sections.title: Reasonable Compensation Warning
sections.content: IRS scrutinizes low salaries. Compensation must be reasonable for services performed. Penalties apply if compensation appears artificially low.

sections.title: Double Tax Effect Example
sections.content: Corporate profit: $100,000 | Corporate tax (21%): ($21,000) | After-tax to distribute: $79,000 | Dividend tax (20%): ($15,800) | Net to shareholder: $63,200 | Effective rate: 36.8%

sections.title: QBI Deduction Overview
sections.content: Section 199A allows up to 20% deduction on qualified business income from pass-through entities. QBI Deduction = 20% × QBI.

sections.title: QBI Above Threshold Limits
rows: Greater of: 50% of W-2 wages OR 25% of wages + 2.5% of qualified property
Specified service trades phase out entirely

sections.title: SSTB Warning
sections.content: SSTBs completely lose QBI deduction above phase-out threshold.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Sole prop: Simple but full SE tax exposure (15.3%)
S Corp: Save SE tax on distributions; must pay reasonable wages
C Corp: 21% entity tax + dividend tax; good for retained earnings
QBI deduction: 20% of pass-through income (limits at high income)
SSTBs (law, accounting, health) lose QBI deduction above threshold

knowledgeCheck.question: Susan operates a sole proprietorship that generated $100,000 in net profit during the year. Her business expenses were accurately reported and deducted. What is the amount of self-employment tax Susan will owe, assuming the Social Security wage base is not exceeded?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Self-employment tax is calculated on 92.35% of your self-employment income. In this case, $100,000 * 0.9235 = $92,350. The self-employment tax rate is 15.3% (12.4% for Social Security and 2.9% for Medicare). Therefore, $92,350 * 0.153 = $14,130.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to reduce your self-employment income by the appropriate percentage before calculating the tax.

reveal.question: What are the 2023 (single filer) thresholds for the Qualified Business Income (QBI) deduction?
reveal.answer:
For 2023, the QBI deduction thresholds for single filers are:

*   **Below:** $182,100
*   **Phase-in Range:** $182,100 - $232,100
*   **Above:** $232,100

rows.label: Taxation of Profits
values: Double Taxation: Profits taxed at corporate level and again when distributed as dividends to shareholders.
Pass-Through Taxation: Profits are not taxed at the corporate level but are passed through to shareholders and taxed at their individual income tax rates.

rows.label: Shareholder Salary
values: Shareholder salaries are deductible business expenses.
Shareholder salaries are deductible business expenses; however, shareholders must take a 'reasonable' salary to avoid reclassification of salary as dividends.

rows.label: Self-Employment Tax
values: No self-employment tax on dividends received.
No self-employment tax on dividends received; only on reasonable salary received.

title: Education Tax Benefits
description: Compare education tax credits and deductions and coordinate benefits to maximize tax savings.
topics: Compare education tax credits and deductions
Coordinate education benefits to maximize tax savings

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Multiple tax benefits exist for education expenses. Understanding their requirements and coordination is key.

sections.title: Expense Adjustments
sections.content: Must reduce expenses by: Tax-free scholarships, Tax-free 529/Coverdell distributions, Other tax-free educational assistance.

sections.title: 529 Superfunding
sections.content: Can contribute 5 years of annual exclusion at once: $95,000 single / $190,000 married (2026). Must not give additional gifts to beneficiary for 5 years.

sections.title: Coordination Rules - No Double-Dipping
sections.content: Same expenses cannot qualify for multiple benefits: Credit AND 529 distribution on same expense, Credit AND employer educational assistance on same expense.

sections.title: Coordination Example
sections.content: Student has $15,000 expenses: $4,000 paid from savings → $2,500 AOTC credit | $11,000 from 529 → tax-free distribution. Total tax benefit: $2,500 credit + tax-free 529.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: AOTC: $2,500/student, 4 years undergrad, 40% refundable
LLC: $2,000/return, unlimited years, grad school OK
529: Tax-free growth, qualified distributions; K-12 ($10K) + higher ed
Don't double-dip: Same expenses can't get credit + 529 + other benefits
Strategy: Use taxable funds for credit, 529 for remaining expenses

title: Tax Credits and Deductions
description: Distinguish refundable from non-refundable credits and maximize available deductions and credits.
topics: Apply child-related tax credits and phase-outs
Utilize retirement savings and other tax credits

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Tax credits provide dollar-for-dollar tax reduction—more valuable than deductions of equal amount.

sections.title: CTC Phase-out Rule
sections.content: Credit reduces by $50 per $1,000 over threshold. Other Dependents Credit: $500/qualifying relative or older dependent (non-refundable, same phase-out).

sections.title: EIC Key Requirements
items: Valid SSN for taxpayer, spouse, children

sections.title: Saver's Credit Rate by Income (2024 MFJ)
Premium Tax Credit Eligibility
items: Not eligible for employer coverage or Medicare

sections.title: Foreign Tax Credit
rows: US tax on foreign source income

sections.title: Credit Planning Strategies
items: Time income: Stay below phase-out thresholds
Track all expenses: Dependent care, adoption

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Credits > Deductions: Dollar-for-dollar vs. marginal rate
Refundable credits create refund if exceeding tax (EIC, partial CTC, AOTC)
Child Tax Credit: $2,000/child under 17; $1,700 refundable; high phase-out
Saver's Credit: 10-50% of up to $2,000 contributions if income qualifies
EIC: Largest refundable credit for low-income workers with children

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements BEST describes the key difference between a refundable and a non-refundable tax credit?
options: A refundable credit can reduce your tax liability to zero, and you can receive the excess as a refund, while a non-refundable credit can only reduce your tax liability to zero.
A refundable credit is always larger in dollar amount than a non-refundable credit.
A non-refundable credit can be carried forward to future tax years if not fully utilized, while a refundable credit cannot.
A refundable credit is only available to taxpayers with dependent children, while a non-refundable credit is available to all taxpayers.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A refundable credit can reduce your tax liability to zero, and if the credit exceeds your tax liability, you will receive the difference as a refund. A non-refundable credit can only reduce your tax liability to zero; you won't receive any of the credit back as a refund.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what happens when your tax liability is already zero before applying the credit.

reveal.question: What is the primary factor that causes the Child Tax Credit to be reduced or eliminated for some taxpayers?
reveal.answer: The Child Tax Credit is subject to income-based phase-outs. As a taxpayer's Modified Adjusted Gross Income (MAGI) exceeds certain thresholds, the amount of the credit is reduced or eliminated entirely. The specific phase-out rules and income thresholds can change from year to year.

sections.title: Saver's Credit vs. Traditional IRA Deduction

rows.label: Type
values: Tax Credit
Tax Deduction

rows.label: Impact
values: Dollar-for-dollar reduction of tax liability
Reduces adjusted gross income (AGI)

rows.label: Eligibility
values: Low-to-moderate income taxpayers
Generally, anyone contributing to a Traditional IRA (subject to limitations if covered by a retirement plan at work)

rows.label: Benefit Calculation
values: Calculated as a percentage of retirement contributions (up to a certain limit)
Amount of the contribution (up to annual limits)

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is an example of an 'above-the-line' deduction (deduction for adjusted gross income)?
options: Itemized deductions for medical expenses exceeding 7.5% of AGI.
Deduction for student loan interest payments.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Above-the-line deductions are subtracted from gross income to arrive at adjusted gross income (AGI). The deduction for student loan interest payments is an example of this type of deduction. The standard deduction and itemized deductions are taken after AGI is calculated.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which deductions are taken *before* calculating AGI.

title: Tax Planning Strategies and Timing
description: Apply income shifting and timing strategies and develop year-end tax planning approaches.
topics: Apply income shifting and timing strategies
Integrate tax planning with financial planning

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Effective tax planning involves timing income and deductions to minimize lifetime taxes.

sections.title: Bunching Strategy
sections.content: Alternate years to exceed standard deduction threshold. Concentrate deductible expenses in alternating years to maximize tax benefit.

sections.title: Bunching Example
sections.content: Year 1: Natural itemized $11,000, prepay $8,000 = $19,000 itemized. Year 2: Natural itemized $11,000 reduced by $8,000 = Standard ($15,350). Without bunching: Standard both years = $30,700. With bunching: $19,000 + $15,350 = $34,350. Extra deduction: $4,400.

sections.title: Donor Advised Fund (DAF) Strategy
sections.content: How it works: 1) Contribute lump sum to DAF in high-income year. 2) Get full deduction in contribution year. 3) Distribute to charities over time. 4) Growth is tax-free.

sections.title: DAF Example
sections.content: Goal: Give $10,000/year to charity. Traditional: $10,000 deduction annually (may not exceed standard). DAF: Contribute $50,000 in Year 1, distribute $10,000/year. Year 1: $50,000 deduction (exceeds standard by $35,400). Years 2-5: Standard deduction.

sections.title: Kiddie Tax
sections.content: Unearned income of children taxed at parent's rate. Applies to children under 19 (or under 24 if student). 2024: First $1,300 tax-free, next $1,300 at child's rate. Excess at parent's rate.

sections.title: Year-End Tax Planning Checklist - Before December 31
Roth Conversion Example
sections.content: Early retirement (low income): Convert $50K/year, fill 12% bracket. Later (with RMDs + SS): No conversion, would be 24% bracket. Net effect: Pay 12% now, avoid 24%+ later.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Defer income when expecting lower future rates; accelerate when opposite
Bunch deductions in alternating years to exceed standard threshold
DAF enables large deduction now with charitable giving over time
Character conversion: Hold >1 year for LTCG; qualified dividends require 60 days
Year-end checklist: Retirement contributions, tax-loss harvest, RMDs, Roth conversions

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following strategies is MOST effective for shifting income to a lower-taxed family member to reduce overall family tax liability, assuming all other requirements are met?
options: Investing in tax-exempt municipal bonds in the higher-taxed family member's name.
Gifting appreciated stock to a child under 18, who then sells the stock.
Establishing a Uniform Transfers to Minors Act (UTMA) account for a child funded with income-producing assets.
Paying a reasonable salary to a child working in the family business.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Paying a reasonable salary to a child for work performed in a family business is the most effective strategy. It shifts income to the child, who is likely in a lower tax bracket, and the business can deduct the salary as a business expense.  Gifting appreciated stock to a child under 18 will result in the kiddie tax applying to unearned income over a certain threshold, which may negate the tax benefit. UTMA accounts are also subject to the kiddie tax rules. Investing in municipal bonds benefits the higher-taxed family member directly, not shifting income to another.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the 'kiddie tax' rules and deductibility for the business.

reveal.question: Explain the primary goal of bunching itemized deductions.
reveal.answer: The primary goal of bunching itemized deductions is to exceed the standard deduction in a given year, allowing the taxpayer to itemize deductions and reduce their taxable income. This strategy is most effective when itemized deductions are close to the standard deduction amount. By concentrating deductions in one year, the taxpayer can itemize, and then take the standard deduction in the following year.

rows.label: Definition
values: Postponing the payment of taxes to a later date.
Using legal methods to minimize or eliminate tax liability.

rows.label: Examples
values: Contributing to a 401(k) or traditional IRA, investing in annuities.
Claiming all eligible deductions and credits, investing in tax-advantaged accounts.

rows.label: Legality
values: Legal
Legal
